speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, So there was this space. Tell me more about how tight this space was.

speaker: interviewee
text: The space was maybe-- I have to-- 50-- the-- the height was of course there, because it was the height of the basement. But the space that we were in was maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what should I say-- can I say 40, 50 centimeters, or 30 inches, something. We were standing up. We were standing up, my sister and myself. We were standing up all night and having only a tiny little hole in the bottom of this space where we had to crawl through and which they had later on closed. Only left a tiny little hole, so that if the Germans really came to the house to-- because somebody had told the Germans, yes, there were Jews in this particular house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- they had Tima and his family had put wooden blocks in front of this tiny little hole and to make sure that the Germans wouldn't see the little hole. Fortunately enough for us, the Germans did not come to the house. So we were OK that evening. And we-- we stayed in there most of the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was on a Saturday evening. It was January and-- oh no. I think, yeah. It was January. I think it was January 11, 1944. It was raining cats and dogs. It was unheard of. It was very, very dark. And we had not been outside for almost a year. Here is my sister, who can hardly see anything, and definitely cannot ride a bicycle at that time of day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was. I am 4' 11'. So that's not very tall. I weigh a little bit more at the time, I think, but about what I weigh now, 85, 90 pounds. I was given-- and we just-- you cannot call it a highlight, but it was the worst time. I was given a tandem bike. I was the chauffeur or the driver of the tandem bike with my sister in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we rode-- and Ali and Tima again, the same couple who had transported us once before, were transported us back almost to the-- to my hometown. Very close-- close to my hometown. So what that meant another 25 kilometers. Same-- we did not go back the same way, because Tima thought that we would be very, very safe. It was raining so hard, we would cross the pontoon bridge, and we didn't have to take the row boat, that was his opinion, that would go faster. I was so scared. I hadn't been outdoors. I hadn't done a thing for a year. We did-- Ali was in front, and she had, let's say, for-- for-- she was on a regular bike. Then I came on the tandem bike with my sister, and Tima followed up in back of us. How I got across the bridge, I do not know. I was so nervous, of course, because on both sides, in the little guard houses, were the Germans. But we made it across, and we made it all the way. The 25 miles-- actually, excuse me, 25 kilometers, and we traveled-- and I remember we-- we went on roads that were, of course, not very much traveled on. By that time, it was Saturday evening, about 10 o'clock. But since it was raining so hard, there were not that many people on the street. And I remember that Tima came close to me. He came from the background, let's say, and he came close to me, and he said, you're almost there, Betty. And we hadn't said a word all these hours. Nobody had spoken to each other, because they think of being-- you know, if anything would have happened, they would have gone, and we would have been picked up. I didn't want anything to-- nobody wanted anything to happen to them. They had risked their lives already enough. So he came finally to me, and he said, Betty you're almost there. And I said, well, if I have to get off this bike, I'm going to fall, I can tell you that already. He said, you're not going to fall. Just wait a second. And he traveled-- he rode his bike for maybe 10, 15, 20 feet, 20 yards ahead of me, and threw his bike on the ground and waited for me and caught me in his arms. And I did not fall. He caught-- caught the bicycle, my tandem bike. And that was very close to the house where we spent our second year in hiding. The people-- this was a-- a-- what should I say-- a farmhouse. An elderly couple in their '80s. And the first one we saw was my brother, Harry. He had been there already maybe a month in that same house. The people, I would like to describe them to you. In their '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember their-- we-- we called them Opa-- Opa means grandpa-- Garben was their last name. Garben.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Garben. G-A-R-B-E-N. And the lady was at least in the 80s. And she didn't really know what was going on, but she didn't have to know, because everybody else took care of us. We came into the house. It was a very old farmhouse. And in the house was also their son and daughter. Their son, his name was Yupe, and the girl's name was Agnes. You couldn't find better people on this earth. Fortunately enough for us, and for me, their name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And their name is also, from what I just heard from Albert Stern, the pilot, their name is also in the Museum in Washington. I have not seen it. I intend to make a special trip to make sure that their names are there. They deserve everything that they could have. We came inside the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CHUCKLES] We were given porridge. You know, whatever you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hot cereal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hot cereal. I was too tired to lift a spoon to my mouth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Logic. I had been holding this heavy, heavy bike, not only with myself on it, but also with my sister on it, right. And Agnes fed me and put me to bed. To bed. I hadn't seen a bed-- oh yeah, I'd seen a bed. But they had a real old-fashioned, you know, in the wall. Bed in a wall. I do not know how you call it here. I've never seen it since I left Holland. The bed was like a closet in the wall. And that's where they put me that night. Me and my sister. My brother, I think, went to the barn. And this is how we slept, and Ali and Tima stayed with us that evening. I think they stayed, but I am not sure. But I would say it was too late for them to come back, anyway. To go back to their own house.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what these people-- I mean, where do you find people who do things like that for you? Shall I tell you now how we lived here? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If there are a few things that you might not like. God forgive me. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 80 feet away from the house, straight through a yard, garden, I would call it, with fruit trees and so on and so forth. That was the garden of the family Garben. Was a old house that only was used as a-- to-- to put the cows and-- as a barn. Was only used as a barn. Sorry if I sometimes forget the English words. It was used as a barn, and they had maybe 15 cows, or maybe 10 cows. And the front of the house-- the house had been empty for about eight years. And this is where we were going to stay. That was going to be our hiding place. What we did the very first day with Agnes and her brother-- and these were people, Agnes was probably in her mid '40s, or maybe in early '40s, and Yupe was definitely in his mid '40s, or maybe even a little bit older. Real farmers. The way that I knew them. Would chew tobacco, you know, that kind of thing. And wooden shoes. And what we did in the front part of the house, we put like a telephone pole, that kind of a-- we put poles from the floor to the ceiling. We did that on purpose so that it looked that the house was almost falling, falling apart. And the ceiling had-- it was a ceiling that had beams. It was decided that if everything was OK, and we felt secure, although we were only four miles away from the German border, but still, they thought we were pretty safe. If we would just-- during the day, we would be able to sit in one of the little rooms and be able to lock our room from the inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: The outside of this barn had a huge padlock, so nobody could come into the barn itself without being-- one of the Garbens being there with the key. Well, they knew what they were doing. So what we did, we took the ceiling, the ceiling of this, let's say, front living room, and we-- between two-- how should I call them? Beams.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beams. We took four slats out. Made that-- on the inside with a screw, made it one square whatever, and that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brought us onto the attic. The attic was not a regular attic. The attic was about six feet of straw and hay.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we did, we dug a hole from where we went in about a yard high, maybe a little bit more, and as tall as my brother. The length of my brother. The width of maybe the table over there, a regular table. And this then was no straw that over our heads was at least five feet of straw, and surrounding us was nothing else but straw. We would crawl up there at night. Yupe Garben would take a ladder. We would go up there-- if it were safe, OK? We would go there, and he would help us getting there. We would screw the four slats, and we would put a blanket on top of that, and that was our bedroom for a year, or a little bit more than a year. So if people now ask me, oh, you cannot sleep in a chair, like they did when my husband just was in the hospital, I can sleep on the floor any place. I can sleep any place, and I can do anything. If you sleep on a place like this for more than a year-- we did have a blanket on the bottom as like a mattress, and we had plenty of blankets to cover us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The room was-- it was not a room, of course, that was a hole. It was a hole-- and-- it was absolutely a hole. And what we did, my brother and my sister slept with their heads one direction, and I slept the opposite side. My feet were almost in their mouth, OK. My feet. So if you had to turn around, you had to do it in unison. The worst part, of course, was bathroom. We had none. We did have a little potty, but you had to be very, very careful, and we had an awful lot of rats, also. And But we got rid of the rats. They were-- later on, they was not the biggest problem. You couldn't go to the bathroom unless you absolutely, absolutely had to, because even the smell would be-- I'm-- I'm being very crude, but the smell would be so that if the Germans really would come and-- and search the barn, we would be found out within a second. The food would be brought by Agnes. She would walk through the yard, and she-- her-- the food would be in a bucket. On the floor of the bucket. And on the top of the bucket would be the food for the chickens who were surrounding our barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were very inventive. We did everything. I mean, you wouldn't believe all the things we did. But that was one of the things. That's how she would bring our food, and if we were very lucky, we would get also a bucket of water maybe once or twice a week in order to be able to wash.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately enough for the girls, under circumstances like that, you don't get a period. Makes it very nice. We did not get a period. But we got something else. We got lice. Lice. Because with us there sometimes on this-- in the-- on the attic, or whatever you want to call it, were cats who had kittens and who would sleep on clothing. And I remember distinctly, [CHUCKLES] Agnes was very, very nice, and she was actually-- my brother got very sick. He turned out to have rheumatoid arthritis, or some arthritis, at least. And Agnes, it was possible-- it was possible for her to get our doctor from home. And he came, and everybody-- this whole little town, there 60, 60, 70 houses, and-- it was not a town. It was a little village. All farms. And I think quite a few people knew that we were there. Half of the people were in the underground. My brother was the one who got the messages from the BBC. He would translate. We were the only one who spoke English a little bit. I learned English a little bit more, because one of the things that bothered me a great deal was Albert Stern, the pilot, had said many times, he used the word [YIDDISH] Since I do not-- I do not speak Yiddish, OK. And I didn't know what the word [YIDDISH] meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked my friend Tima, bring me a dictionary, a Dutch-English dictionary. I've got to find this word. I really-- I understood what he meant, but I didn't really to know. I took my English-Dutch dictionary a hundred times over. I thought, how many different ways can you spell this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't find it anyplace. Then, of course, after the war, I found out that [YIDDISH] was a Yiddish word, and it meant approximately. And I didn't know that. Those are the things that, of course, we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you doing there all day, every day? You couldn't sew anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But do I-- can I knit. I-- during that time, my sister would also knit. I knitted something like between 30 and 35 sweaters. And I was able to knit a sock per day. [CHUCKLES] I wouldn't be able to do it now, but at the time. We did all kinds of things. We were not in this little hole all day and night long. Sometimes we would be in the little room, and there are two things that I wanted to tell you that happened. I told you already how difficult it was to go to the bathroom. We went to the bathroom where the cows were. Where the-- you know, and whatever. That meant we had to go through another room into the barn part and go where the cows were and if--

speaker: interviewer
text: To mix the smells.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. So that they wouldn't have our smell and so on and so forth. And we did that many a times. One day, and I think it was in July of 1944, my sister had gone to the bathroom. On her way back, just in the room right in-- before, where we were in hiding, if it was during the day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Agnes took her-- there was a German soldier, or somebody German. There were a lot of German soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wanted to see the inside of the barn. And we were-- my brother and I were in this little room. And my sister wasn't there. So we knew she could only be in the room next to ours. And when this German fellow would walk in, he would see her standing there. And that would be the end of all of us, and we knew this, OK? So I hugged my brother like the last minute of my life. Agnes, I can hear her. The only thing in this room was an-- you know, what an armoire. In Holland, they didn't have closets built in. Here is this German fellow, and he is pestering Agnes. He wants to buy the armoire.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to sell it to me, it's a beautiful antique. She said, no. I don't want to sell it. It's the only one I have. I don't have anything at home anymore because we were already hit with cannon fire and whatever. So this is the only place where I have some of my clothes in here. No, I'm not going to sell you the armoire. And they stood there and argued for about 10 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, the two of us, my brother and I, we couldn't figure out how come my sister wasn't standing right there? Where was she? She had to be someplace, because I had heard the door open and close. The only thing I could think was maybe she was in the fireplace. She had, you know, maybe that's where she was hiding. But I couldn't think how she could have doing that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, 10 minutes later, Agnes and this German fellow, they leave the room. I opened the little room where we were in. I opened the door maybe an inch. The door of the armoire opens up, and my sister steps out. So you can just only imagine. That was one-- that was-- that, I would say, plus our bike tour on the-- on the tandem, those were the things that-- you know, there are-- there are more.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no heating. Of course not. I had terribly frostbitten hands, unbelievable. So what do you do? You-- you knit yourself a pair of gloves, right? Only the tips I didn't knit. So my gloves came to here, and I was able still to do my knitting, but at least it would keep me warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was early, I think, in the summer, and the roof of this house that we were in had a big hole in it. You know, that didn't matter, you know, the straw could get wet, it was OK. And I climbed-- some days were a little bit calmer, when we knew. See, the police and everybody underground, and everybody knew that we were there. If there was anything coming, as far as Germans was concerned-- this was way out in the sticks-- they would know it before anything would happen, and they would come and warn us. So when it was really quiet, they would say, oh, you can stay downstairs today. Today should be a good day for you. So we did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, I was so-- my hands were so terrible, and I was so cold, that I said, you know what? I'm going to climb on the attic. And I climbed up with a ladder on the attic where the cows were. All the cows had names by the way, and we had trained them all very, very well. [CHUCKLES] We did everything. We-- we helped them do everything. But I climbed-- What do you mean you trained them? What do you mean you trained cows?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were eight-- there were eight spots for the cows to lie down on. We had 10 cows. So Betsy had to get up. Betsy, come on, get up. So that Connie could lie down. You know, that kind of thing. So we-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they respond to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- very much so. Very much so. So we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, do you think of them as intelligent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know, but for us, there was the only things that we-- only people, almost, where we can talk to. We couldn't talk to anybody. I must say, we talked to each other a lot. We talked to each other a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very close. I call my relatives every weekend. So [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go on to liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm going to tell you about the-- so I climb on the attic, and I told my sister to come also, and she did. No problem. But then when we went back from the attic, there was a space-- and I told you my sister had very, very poor eyesight. There was a space between the upper attic and the lower one. And I jumped. It was maybe a yard. And I jumped, and I was OK. I got to the lower portion, and I climbed down on the ladder that I had standing there. My sister didn't jump far enough, so she fell off the attic and came down on the floor. Fortunately enough, she didn't break anything. But the main thing was her glasses, her glasses. Fortunately enough, even the glasses did not break. The glasses did not break.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that whole thing-- I mean, it was, of course, a hassle and everything, but it was-- while we were there-- and the other thing, before I forget that, two more American fliers stayed with us. If you want to know the words to American Air Force song, you can ask me, because I learned that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the three American air people, Air Force people who stayed with us, two of them happened to be Jewish. One was Albert Stern, who now lives in San Diego. The other one is Carl Glassman. He was more my size. He was not very, very tall. He also was Jewish. He was a surgeon later on in Delaware, and I-- among my belongings I have a letter from him that he wrote me while I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and the third one was very, very tall and heavy, and his name was [INAUDIBLE]. I don't remember his first name. But that was the day when this airplane to me-- this airplane came down, and of course, Germans all over the place. So what do you do? Instead of three people in our little hiding place, we had five people in our little hiding place. You couldn't move, but my, were we ever safe. We were safe. Nobody found us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The underground then later on, after a couple of days, came to pick up the two Americans. And they were smuggled through Holland. This was-- I do not know exactly what time it was. I think-- oh, I think it was in early April, or-- I think they were there-- it was my-- my birthday. My birthday is in April. So I learned more English. They were so good in telling us all these-- and to tell us just, to write down the words to Air Corp song. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was no romance between you guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I was-- no, not at all. I-- i don't think-- that reminds me of something that you just said. Yupe, Agnes, her brother had never gotten married. And one evening, they came to pick up all the people, the men, between let's say, 25 and 50. And Yupe came in our hiding place. He was also hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his father, who was in his '80s, their father was-- yeah, at least 80 to 82, or something like that, came to me, and he said-- and Yupe came to sleep with us, right? So his father said, that's the first and only time that Yupe slept with a woman. [CHUCKLES] So that was really-- we laughed a lot about things like that. The Garben people are-- I can tell you six books, as far as that's concerned. They did everything for us. Everything. Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Canadian and-- I think the actual soldiers-- Canadian and English soldiers. I do not know what. We had a terrible time just a couple of days beforehand, because we were actually, I think, only about four miles away from the river Rhine. And the Americans had to cross, and whoever the troops were, the Allies had to cross the Rhine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was-- in a-- on a distance, or in a-- let's say in a-- 3,600 cannons blasted for hours [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just to get across the Rhine. And the only thing that I remember that we were liberated was-- it was-- all of a sudden, it was-- oh, we had a machine gun, German machine gun, right in front of the barn that we were in, OK? For the last couple of days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the last evening of-- what turned out to be the last evening of the actual war that we had-- I could not stay upstairs on the attic. I was so afraid and so scared, and the house next to us was hit and burned down. So I just slid down a rope and sat actually maybe 10 feet away from the German soldiers. Because-- I was indoors. The soldiers were outdoors. I was so afraid that if something happened, I would burn to a crisp, and I couldn't take that. So that was what happened that evening. And then the following morning, when everything was quiet, one of our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] underground people came by and whistled a song, 'In Name From Orange,' and the Queen of Holland is the House of Orange. In the Name of the House of Orange, open the door. You're liberated. That's what he said.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother went outside immediately. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed indoors at least three more days. I was-- see, for more than two years, we had trying to hide from everybody. You couldn't-- you know, if your face showed any place, that brought the people in danger who were hiding us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt just like the nuns who had taught me how to play the piano. The nuns were also very-- you know, they-- they were not used to regular life with people and being on the street, and so on and so forth. This was a very strict order, the-- the nuns that taught me how to play the piano.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after more than two years being indoors, I wouldn't go out. And when I did-- when I did go out, when I saw the first person, I hid behind some bushes. And that was the story.
